0.title: Knowledge Hub Foundations
0.abstract: Knowledge Hub Foundations curates the first expansion wave of Electronic Artefacts knowledge records around graph systems, preservation, generative systems, signal, audio and public semantic publishing.

1.title: Knowledge Hub Second Wave
1.abstract: Knowledge Hub Second Wave groups a larger expansion of Electronic Artefacts knowledge records across hypertext, open source, metadata, creative coding, web audio, motion, typography, HCI, algorithmic music and generative AI.

2.title: Knowledge Hub Third Wave
2.abstract: Knowledge Hub Third Wave groups a major expansion across LLMs, AI agents, local AI, RAG, multimodal systems, systems thinking, ontologies, graph modeling, event streams, Redis, microservices and domain runtimes.

3.title: AI Agent
3.definition: An AI agent is a software system in which a model can select actions, use tools, observe results and adapt its next steps toward an objective within defined boundaries.
3.abstract: AI agents combine models, tools, memory, environmental feedback, stopping conditions, permissions and evaluation in an iterative action loop.

4.title: Algorithmic Composition
4.definition: Algorithmic composition is the use of rules, procedures, probability, computation or formal systems to generate or organize musical material.
4.abstract: Algorithmic composition connects music theory, computation, cybernetics, generative systems and electronic music by treating composition as the design of procedures as well as sounds.

5.title: Augmented Intelligence
5.definition: Augmented intelligence is the design of tools, interfaces and systems that expand human perception, reasoning, memory and creative capability.
5.abstract: Augmented intelligence reframes computation as a partner in human work, linking HCI, AI, knowledge systems, creative tools and Electronic Artefacts runtime research.

6.title: Autonomous System
6.definition: An autonomous system can perceive relevant state, select actions and adapt behavior toward objectives with limited direct human control during operation.
6.abstract: Autonomous systems combine sensing, models, planning, execution, feedback, constraints, governance and human intervention paths.

7.title: Contextual Execution
7.definition: Contextual execution is the practice of making identity, state, permissions, relationships and operating context active constraints on what a system can do.
7.abstract: Contextual execution describes runtime behavior where an operation is evaluated through entity identity, graph relations, permissions, temporal state and intended scope.

8.title: Creative Coding
8.definition: Creative coding is the use of programming as an artistic, design or exploratory medium rather than only as an engineering implementation tool.
8.abstract: Creative coding connects software, visual culture, sound, interaction, generative systems, education and experimental publishing.

9.title: Cybernetic Feedback
9.definition: Cybernetic feedback is the circulation of information about a system's state back into the system so that behavior can be regulated, adapted or transformed.
9.abstract: Cybernetic feedback connects control, communication, systems theory, generative art, machine listening, adaptive interfaces and runtime governance.

10.title: Digital Preservation
10.definition: Digital preservation is the long-term work of keeping digital objects understandable, authentic, usable and accessible despite changes in formats, software, hardware and context.
10.abstract: Digital preservation connects file formats, metadata, provenance, storage, rights, migration and interpretation into a long-term care practice for digital culture.

11.title: Domain Operating System
11.definition: A domain operating system is a shared runtime and information layer that coordinates identities, entities, permissions, workflows, events and interfaces for a specific field of activity.
11.abstract: A domain operating system turns repeated domain rules into reusable infrastructure without claiming to replace a general-purpose computer operating system.

12.title: Entity Identity
12.definition: Entity identity is the durable naming of a person, concept, project, program, publication or artefact so that references remain stable as descriptions evolve.
12.abstract: Entity identity gives knowledge systems a way to cite things without confusing names, pages, files, versions and changing descriptions.

13.title: Event-Driven Architecture
13.definition: Event-driven architecture is a system design approach in which components publish, transport and react to records of state change rather than depending only on direct synchronous calls.
13.abstract: Event-driven architecture connects event producers, brokers or logs, consumers, schemas, delivery semantics, projections and observability.

14.title: Generative AI
14.definition: Generative AI refers to machine-learning systems that produce or transform media such as text, image, audio, video, code or structured data from learned patterns.
14.abstract: Generative AI connects machine learning, creative workflows, latent spaces, risk management, provenance, authorship and cultural production.

15.title: Generative System
15.definition: A generative system is a rule-based, procedural or computational arrangement that produces variable outputs from defined constraints, materials and processes.
15.abstract: Generative systems connect creative coding, cybernetics, algorithmic composition, simulation, design tools and AI by shifting authorship toward rules, feedback and selection.

16.title: Graph Modeling
16.definition: Graph modeling represents a domain through addressable nodes, typed relationships and properties chosen to answer specific questions and support specific operations.
16.abstract: Graph modeling makes many-to-many relationships, paths, provenance and evolving context explicit where folders and rigid trees obscure them.

17.title: Graph Runtime
17.definition: A graph runtime is an execution environment in which addressable entities and typed relationships participate directly in contextual computation.
17.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses graph runtime to describe systems where entities, relationships, identity, context and events form an executable structure rather than a passive data model.

18.title: Human Computer Interaction
18.definition: Human computer interaction is the study and design of relationships between people, computational systems, interfaces, tasks, environments and cultures of use.
18.abstract: HCI connects interface design, accessibility, creative tools, augmented intelligence, software ergonomics and the social consequences of computation.

19.title: Hypertext
19.definition: Hypertext is a system of non-linear text and media connected by links, allowing readers and machines to traverse knowledge through associations.
19.abstract: Hypertext is a foundational concept for the web, digital archives, knowledge graphs, digital gardens, public notes and Electronic Artefacts knowledge publishing.

20.title: Internet Culture
20.definition: Internet culture is the set of social forms, aesthetics, protocols, communities, memes, archives and practices that emerge through networked communication.
20.abstract: Internet culture connects network protocols, web publishing, platform communities, memes, archives, open source, digital folklore and public knowledge systems.

21.title: Knowledge Graph
21.definition: A knowledge graph is a structured network of entities, identifiers and typed relationships that makes knowledge navigable, queryable and reusable across contexts.
21.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses knowledge graph to describe public knowledge systems where concepts, projects, programs, publications, sources and artefacts keep stable identities and typed relations.

22.title: Large Language Model
22.definition: A large language model is a parameterized machine-learning model trained to predict and generate sequences of tokens from statistical patterns learned across large text or multimodal corpora.
22.abstract: Large language models combine tokenization, embeddings, transformer layers, attention, training objectives and probabilistic decoding to generate or transform language.

23.title: Linked Data
23.definition: Linked Data is a web publishing approach that identifies things with stable URIs and connects them through machine-readable statements.
23.abstract: Linked Data gives public knowledge systems a way to make entities, pages, references and datasets interoperable across sites and software systems.

24.title: Metadata
24.definition: Metadata is structured information about a resource, such as title, creator, date, format, source, rights, subject, relation and preservation context.
24.abstract: Metadata is the descriptive, administrative, structural and preservation layer that makes archives, catalogues, digital objects and knowledge graphs usable over time.

25.title: Microservice Architecture
25.definition: Microservice architecture structures a software system as independently deployable services aligned with bounded capabilities and communicating through explicit contracts.
25.abstract: Microservices can improve independent evolution and scaling but add network, data, deployment, observability and organizational complexity.

26.title: Motion Design
26.definition: Motion design is the use of time, movement, transition, rhythm and sequencing to structure visual communication and interface behavior.
26.abstract: Motion design connects visual culture, interface semantics, typography, animation, sound, choreography and web interaction.

27.title: Multimodal AI
27.definition: Multimodal AI describes systems that learn from, align, transform or generate more than one modality such as text, image, audio, video, sensor data or structured records.
27.abstract: Multimodal AI connects representation learning, modality encoders, shared embedding spaces, generation, alignment and cross-modal interfaces.

28.title: Ontology
28.definition: In knowledge engineering, an ontology is an explicit specification of the kinds of entities, properties, relationships and constraints used to represent a domain.
28.abstract: Ontologies make domain meaning explicit and interoperable, while taxonomies organize concepts primarily through classification and hierarchy.

29.title: Open Source
29.definition: Open source is a software and cultural practice in which source code is made available under licenses that allow use, study, modification and redistribution.
29.abstract: Open source is both a licensing model and a cultural infrastructure for software, creative tools, public knowledge, maintainership and technical education.

30.title: Open-Weight Model
30.definition: An open-weight model is a machine-learning model whose learned parameters are distributed for reuse under stated terms, without necessarily providing the training data, training code or freedoms required by open-source definitions.
30.abstract: Open-weight models enable local inference and adaptation but must be distinguished from fully open-source AI systems.

31.title: Personal Knowledge System
31.definition: A personal knowledge system is a practice and tool environment for capturing, linking, revising and retrieving notes, sources, ideas and decisions over time.
31.abstract: Personal knowledge systems connect digital gardens, wikis, note archives, research methods, backlinks and long-term intellectual memory.

32.title: Procedural Graphics
32.definition: Procedural graphics are images or visual systems generated from rules, algorithms, parameters, simulation or code rather than only manually drawn assets.
32.abstract: Procedural graphics connect shaders, generative systems, motion design, creative coding, visual culture and real-time web interfaces.

33.title: Provenance
33.definition: Provenance is information about the people, activities, sources, transformations and decisions involved in producing a thing or piece of data.
33.abstract: Provenance helps readers assess reliability, rights, authorship, lineage, context and trust across research, archive, software and cultural records.

34.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
34.definition: Retrieval-augmented generation is an architecture that retrieves external information at query time and supplies selected evidence to a generative model before or during response production.
34.abstract: RAG connects search, embeddings, document segmentation, metadata, provenance, context assembly, language models and answer evaluation.

35.title: Signal Archaeology
35.definition: Signal archaeology is the interpretation of technical, sonic, visual or archival traces as evidence of hidden processes, transformations and histories.
35.abstract: Signal archaeology gives Electronic Artefacts a way to connect audio analysis, media residue, archival provenance, memory and cultural interpretation.

36.title: Systems Thinking
36.definition: Systems thinking is an approach that studies elements through their relationships, feedback, boundaries, delays, stocks, flows and patterns of behavior over time.
36.abstract: Systems thinking helps creators and technologists understand why local interventions can produce indirect, delayed or counterintuitive effects.

37.title: Typography
37.definition: Typography is the design and arrangement of written language through typefaces, spacing, hierarchy, rhythm, scale and reading conditions.
37.abstract: Typography connects design, reading systems, accessibility, visual culture, web interfaces and long-form knowledge publishing.

38.title: Web Audio
38.definition: Web audio is the use of browser-based audio APIs and related web technologies to synthesize, process, analyze, route and present sound.
38.abstract: Web audio connects sound design, browser runtimes, interactive media, creative coding and public sound tools.

39.title: Electronic Artefacts
39.abstract: Electronic Artefacts is an independent creative technology studio, software laboratory, research organization, cultural platform, label and publishing system.

40.title: ORETH
40.abstract: ORETH is an Electronic Artefacts audio intelligence and artistic research program concerned with signal analysis, pattern detection, memory, machine listening and experimental music production.

41.title: VASTE
41.abstract: VASTE is the primary proprietary runtime developed and commercialized by Electronic Artefacts for graph architectures, contextual execution, identity systems and modular world construction.

42.title: Palimpsests
42.abstract: Palimpsests is an Electronic Artefacts cultural project and conceptual album cycle connected to ORETH, memory, inheritance, transmission, entropy, signal and layered artistic production.

43.title: Vestiges
43.abstract: Vestiges is the flagship VASTE platform for mapping, preserving and activating living know-how across people, practices, materials, places and institutions.

44.title: AI Agents vs AI Workflows
44.abstract: A practical comparison of model-directed agents, deterministic workflows, hybrid orchestration, tool permissions, memory, evaluation and human oversight.

45.title: Algorithmic Composition and Rule-Based Music
45.abstract: This article explains algorithmic composition through rules, stochastic methods, cybernetics, electronic music, creative coding, generative systems and ORETH.

46.title: Contextual Execution and Graph Runtimes
46.abstract: This article explains contextual execution as a runtime principle for systems where identity, relations, permissions and state influence what operations can occur.

47.title: Creative Coding Pedagogy from Logo to p5.js
47.abstract: This article explains creative coding pedagogy through exploratory programming, computational literacy, Processing, p5.js, visual feedback and open learning communities.

48.title: Digital Preservation and Living Archives
48.abstract: This article explains digital preservation as an active cultural practice that keeps digital objects, metadata, provenance and interpretation usable over time.

49.title: Event-Driven Architecture and Event Streams
49.abstract: A practical explanation of events, commands, queues, logs, brokers, schemas, delivery semantics, idempotency, projections, replay and observability.

50.title: Foundational Lineage #001
50.abstract: This research note records early architecture, communication-system and documentation threads that led to Electronic Artefacts, Runtime Theory and VASTE.

51.title: Generative AI, Latent Space and Creative Workflows
51.abstract: This article explains generative AI as a creative workflow topic, covering latent spaces, prompts, provenance, risk, evaluation, authorship and Electronic Artefacts systems.

52.title: Generative Systems, Cybernetics and Creative Coding
52.abstract: This article traces generative systems from cybernetic feedback and algorithmic art to creative coding, sound design and contemporary AI-assisted production.

53.title: How Large Language Models Actually Work
53.abstract: A grounded explanation of how large language models turn tokens into predictions through embeddings, transformer layers, attention, training and probabilistic inference.

54.title: Human Computer Interaction for Creative Tools
54.abstract: This article explains HCI for creative tools, focusing on feedback, agency, flow, error recovery, accessibility, augmented intelligence and computational media.

55.title: Hypertext and Augmented Knowledge Systems
55.abstract: This article explains hypertext as a long-term knowledge architecture, connecting Vannevar Bush, Douglas Engelbart, web links, knowledge graphs and augmented intelligence.

56.title: Knowledge Graphs for Cultural Infrastructure
56.abstract: This article explains how knowledge graphs support cultural infrastructure by giving people, works, sources, concepts, projects and archives stable identities and explicit relations.

57.title: Linked Data and Public Knowledge Pages
57.abstract: This article explains how public knowledge pages can use stable URLs, JSON-LD, RDF-oriented identifiers and internal links to become useful to humans, search engines and AI systems.

58.title: Local and Open Source AI Systems
58.abstract: A practical guide to local inference, open-weight models, open-source AI, quantization, hardware, privacy, licensing and operational independence.

59.title: Metadata, Cataloguing and Cultural Memory
59.abstract: This article explains metadata as a cultural memory layer, connecting cataloguing, Dublin Core, PREMIS, METS, provenance, archives and knowledge graphs.

60.title: Microservices, Modular Monoliths and System Boundaries
60.abstract: A pragmatic comparison of microservices and modular monoliths through domain boundaries, data ownership, deployment, events, observability and team structure.

61.title: Motion Design, Time and Interface Semantics
61.abstract: This article explains motion design as a semantic layer for digital interfaces, connecting time, transition, attention, accessibility and Web Animations.

62.title: Multimodal AI Across Text, Image, Audio and Video
62.abstract: A durable guide to multimodal AI architecture, shared representations, cross-modal retrieval, generation, temporal media, provenance and creative applications.

63.title: Ontologies, Taxonomies and Knowledge Modeling
63.abstract: A practical guide to taxonomies, thesauri, folksonomies, ontologies, controlled vocabularies, OWL, SKOS and sustainable semantic modeling.

64.title: Open Source as Cultural Infrastructure
64.abstract: This article frames open source as cultural infrastructure, explaining licenses, maintenance, creative tools, dependency chains, commons and public knowledge systems.

65.title: Personal Knowledge Systems and Digital Gardens
65.abstract: This article explains personal knowledge systems and digital gardens as durable practices for notes, sources, backlinks, revision, public learning and research libraries.

66.title: Procedural Graphics, Shaders and Visual Systems
66.abstract: This article explains procedural graphics through rules, shaders, parameters, WebGL, generative systems and visual grammars for digital art and interfaces.

67.title: Redis Streams for Orchestration
67.abstract: An implementation-oriented guide to Redis Streams, consumer groups, acknowledgements, pending entries, retries, idempotency, trimming and orchestration boundaries.

68.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Knowledge Systems
68.abstract: An end-to-end explanation of RAG architecture, ingestion, chunking, embeddings, hybrid retrieval, graphs, citations, permissions and evaluation.

69.title: Runtime Engines and Domain Operating Systems
69.abstract: A systems-level guide to runtime engines, domain operating systems, identity, state, permissions, events, extensions and public projections.

70.title: Signal Archaeology, Audio Memory and Machine Listening
70.abstract: This article explains signal archaeology as a way to read sonic and technical traces through audio analysis, provenance, memory and machine listening.

71.title: Systems Thinking for Creative Practice
71.abstract: A practical introduction to systems, boundaries, feedback, stocks, flows, delays, leverage points and their use in creative and technical work.

72.title: Typography, Reading Systems and Digital Interfaces
72.abstract: This article explains typography as infrastructure for digital reading, connecting type, layout, hierarchy, accessibility, CSS Fonts and long-form knowledge publishing.

73.title: Web Audio and Browser-Based Sound Systems
73.abstract: This article explains Web Audio as a browser-native sound architecture for synthesis, processing, analysis, interactive essays, creative coding and public audio tools.

74.title: Why Graphs Are More Powerful Than Folders
74.abstract: A practical comparison of hierarchical folders and graph models for knowledge, projects, archives, identity, provenance and generated interfaces.

75.title: Runtime Theory
75.abstract: Runtime Theory investigates the minimum identities, relations, contexts and event primitives required for a coherent executable system.

76.title: ActivityPub
76.abstract: ActivityPub is a decentralized social networking protocol standardized by W3C for federated server-to-server and client-to-server activity exchange.

77.title: CloudEvents
77.abstract: CloudEvents is a CNCF specification for describing event data in a common, portable envelope.

78.title: CSS Fonts
78.abstract: CSS Fonts defines web font selection, font faces and typographic behavior for digital interfaces and web documents.

79.title: JSON-LD
79.abstract: JSON-LD is a W3C format for serializing Linked Data in JSON, making semantic graph metadata compatible with common web programming workflows.

80.title: llama.cpp
80.abstract: llama.cpp is an open-source C and C++ inference runtime for running large language models across consumer and server hardware.

81.title: OWL
81.abstract: OWL is a W3C ontology language with formally defined semantics for classes, properties, individuals and constraints on the Semantic Web.

82.title: RDF
82.abstract: RDF is a W3C graph data model for representing information as subject-predicate-object statements about resources.

83.title: Redis Streams
83.abstract: Redis Streams is an append-only log data type with ordered entries, range reads, trimming and consumer-group processing.

84.title: SKOS
84.abstract: SKOS is a W3C data model for publishing thesauri, taxonomies, classification schemes and other knowledge organization systems on the Web.

85.title: Transformer Architecture
85.abstract: The transformer is a neural-network architecture built around attention mechanisms and parallel sequence processing.

86.title: Web Animations
86.abstract: Web Animations defines a timing and animation model for changes to web page presentation and related programming interfaces.

87.title: Web Audio API
87.abstract: The Web Audio API is a browser platform technology for routing, synthesizing, processing and analyzing audio through connected audio nodes.

88.title: WebGL
88.abstract: WebGL is a web platform technology for rendering interactive 2D and 3D graphics in compatible browsers using GPU acceleration.
